title: The Checks

jokes.text: Medication for the well.
jokes.setup: Jerry's observation about preventive indigestion drugs creating a new market
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'medication for the well' perfectly encapsulates the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical observation that creates a memorable phrase. Classic Seinfeld observational comedy with excellent wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So now we have medication for the well. That's what these preventive indigestion drugs are. Medication for the well. I mean, think about it. You're not sick. You feel fine. But you take this pill just in case you might get indigestion later. It's medication for the well.

jokes.text: What is this, a bit? No. Because I'm not in the mood.
jokes.setup: Jerry doing his observational routine
jokes.punchline: George's meta-commentary on Jerry treating life like standup material
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's annoyance at Jerry's comedian tendencies.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice how we treat everyday situations like they're material? Like, 'Oh, that's a good bit!'

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, well some of us are actually living our lives instead of narrating them for an audience that doesn't exist.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: An audience that doesn't exist? George, I have a career.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly. You're so busy mining your life for jokes, you're not actually experiencing it. You're like a human laugh track.

jokes.text: If you call that living.
jokes.setup: Furniture salesman says most people never sit in a Farbman
jokes.punchline: George's dramatic overstatement about furniture defining existence
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation and delivery, though the premise is familiar.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Not sit in a Farbman? What are we, animals? We're supposed to sit! Sitting is what separates us from the apes. If we don't sit, what's the point of being human?

jokes.text: Brett not responding during 'Desperado'
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to talk to her boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Brett completely ignoring her because he's absorbed in the song
jokes.explanation: Sets up Brett's odd obsession with the song that becomes a running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Brett, hello? Brett?

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: ♪ She loves you, yeah, yeah, yeah ♪

jokes.text: And you're still dating him?
jokes.setup: Elaine defending Brett being moved by 'Desperado'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's incredulous response to this romantic 'quality'
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Jerry's judgment of Elaine's boyfriends.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He cried at 'Desperado'? What does that tell you about a guy? I'll tell you what it tells you — it tells you he's a weeper. He's a crier. That's not a good quality in a man!

jokes.text: I'll tell you who sounds a little desperado.
jokes.setup: Jerry criticizing her boyfriend choice
jokes.punchline: Elaine turning the song title into an insult
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay but fairly predictable given the song reference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't understand what you see in this guy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What do you mean? He's great!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Great? The man has no ambition, no sense of humor...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, so what are you saying - 'She Drives Me Crazy'?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, maybe you're the one who's crazy. Ever think of that?

jokes.text: That had to be invented?
jokes.setup: Jerry claiming he invented umbrella twirling
jokes.punchline: George's incredulous response to such a simple 'innovation'
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor questioning the banality of Jerry's 'invention'.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You invented umbrella twirling? What, you think you're the first person to spin an umbrella? My father was doing that in 1952!

jokes.text: The man doesn't want a history lesson.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking the umbrella salesman about Jerry's claim
jokes.punchline: Jerry's embarrassed attempt to shut down the conversation
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Jerry's discomfort when caught in a lie.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So you're saying Jerry came in here and bought an umbrella?

dialogue.speaker: Umbrella Salesman
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's right. He said it was for a friend.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He said that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, can we not do this right now? This is not the time or the place.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm just asking about the umbrella, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know what you're asking, and the answer is it's complicated.

jokes.text: In his dreams.
jokes.setup: Umbrella salesman crediting Teddy Padilac with the twirl
jokes.punchline: Jerry's defensive dismissal when his story is contradicted
jokes.explanation: Shows Jerry's stubbornness but the line itself isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Teddy Padilac invented the twirl. I'm telling you.

dialogue.speaker: Other
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes it is! He did it first!

jokes.text: You're doing it too fast. You'll disorient the customers.
jokes.setup: Jerry being proven wrong about inventing the twirl
jokes.punchline: Still giving 'expert' advice on the technique despite being discredited
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Jerry's inability to let go even when wrong is beautifully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no, you're doing it all wrong. You gotta get your wrist up higher. See, the key to a good twirl is all in the wrist.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I invented this move. I know what I'm talking about.

jokes.text: I find it disorienting. Who buys an umbrella anyway? You can get 'em for free in the coffee shop in the metal cans.
jokes.setup: Discussion about umbrella twirling being disorienting
jokes.punchline: Jerry's logic about stealing umbrellas from coffee shops
jokes.explanation: Good observational setup with a punchline that reveals Jerry's casual dishonesty.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I hate? When you're at a coffee shop and someone takes your umbrella by mistake. So I started doing the same thing. I figure, it evens out. I steal their umbrella, they steal mine, we're even. It's like a cosmic balance.

jokes.text: Those belong to people.
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting taking umbrellas from coffee shops
jokes.punchline: George's obvious moral correction delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response. Economy and timing make it work.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, you can't just take the umbrella from the coffee shop.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? They have a whole bucket of them.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because it's not yours. You don't take things that don't belong to you.

jokes.text: Each one is for, like, 12 cents. It's barely worth the pain in my hand to sign them.
jokes.setup: Jerry getting royalty checks from Japanese TV show
jokes.punchline: The absurdly small amounts making the effort almost pointless
jokes.explanation: Great setup for the episode's carpal tunnel running gag, with good absurdist logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm getting these royalty checks from a Japanese TV show. Twelve cents. Nineteen cents. Last week I got a check for three cents.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Three cents! Do you know what that means? Somebody in Japan watched me, and they thought I was worth three cents.

jokes.text: You're on that list.
jokes.setup: George calling Elaine's boyfriends 'losers'
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact reminder that George dated Elaine too
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback delivered with Kramer's characteristic bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you dated her too.

jokes.text: So? For a $25 cleaning, I can listen to some pointless blather. I do it. I'm not even getting the cleaning.
jokes.setup: Kramer warning about cult carpet cleaners
jokes.punchline: George's logic and self-aware final line about his social life
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - George's willingness to be preached to reveals his loneliness perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: These carpet cleaners, they're like a cult. They get in your apartment, they never leave.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? They cleaned my carpet and they left.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but they'll be back. They always come back.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what? Let them come back. At least someone's coming to my apartment.

jokes.text: That's the funny face that greets you at the beginning of The Super Terrific Happy Hour.
jokes.setup: Japanese tourists recognizing Jerry
jokes.punchline: Kramer's description of Jerry as just 'the funny face' in the credits
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with Kramer's casual diminishment of Jerry's fame.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's right. You see, in Japan, they don't know who Jerry is. They just see 'the funny face' in the credits and they go crazy for it.

jokes.text: Everybody laughs at Jerry here in America.
jokes.setup: Trying to build up Jerry's comedy credentials to tourists
jokes.punchline: The phrasing makes it sound like Jerry is the butt of jokes rather than telling them
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection that works both as Kramer's verbal clumsiness and meta-commentary.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm telling people you're a comedian.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean you're telling people I'm a comedian?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, I went down to the coffee shop, I told some tourists you were a comedian.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: To build up your credentials! I said, 'That's Jerry, he's a comedian. He does a bit about airline food.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, that's nice of you, but you can't just go around telling people I'm a comedian.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not? You are a comedian.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, but the way you're saying it makes it sound like people make jokes ABOUT me, not that I tell jokes.

jokes.text: You forgot to sign your check.
jokes.setup: George expecting religious cult recruitment pitch
jokes.punchline: The 'one more thing' is just a forgotten signature
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that subverts George's paranoid expectations.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: So what's this all about? You're not going to try to recruit me into some kind of cult, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Carpet Cleaner
dialogue.line: Cult? No, no. I just need you to sign here.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Oh, okay, good.

dialogue.speaker: Carpet Cleaner
dialogue.line: Oh, one more thing...

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Here it comes.

dialogue.speaker: Carpet Cleaner
dialogue.line: I need your initial right here.

jokes.text: Life can be so confusing. I-I'm... I'm searching for answers anywhere.
jokes.setup: Carpet cleaner provides no cult pitch
jokes.punchline: George desperately trying to get recruited by dropping obvious bait
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - George's desperate need for attention leads him to beg for cult recruitment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about joining a group. You know, some kind of organization where they tell you what to do, what to think, what to wear...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'd be willing to give up my possessions, change my name, move to a compound...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm very open-minded about the whole thing. Very open-minded.

jokes.text: Super-terrific carpal tunnel syndrome.
jokes.setup: Kramer asking about Jerry's claw-like hand
jokes.punchline: Jerry incorporating the Japanese show title into his medical condition
jokes.explanation: Nice wordplay combining the show title with Jerry's physical condition.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What's with your hand?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, it just froze like this. I think I have a condition.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A condition? What kind of condition?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I call it The Karate Kid.

jokes.text: I don't write skits.
jokes.setup: Brett suggesting Jerry needs a desk for writing skits
jokes.punchline: Jerry's flat correction of the diminutive term for his work
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jerry's professional pride and Brett's condescension.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: You need a desk. You're a writer. Writers have desks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not writing skits. I'm writing material.

jokes.text: Of course you don't. You don't have a proper workstation.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying he doesn't write skits
jokes.punchline: Brett's condescending 'logic' about why Jerry doesn't write
jokes.explanation: Perfect Brett character moment - his oblivious condescension is perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I think what it is is that you're afraid. You're afraid that if you sit down and actually try to write something, that it might not be good. And that would be very difficult for you to face. So instead, you just tell people that you don't write.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, I just don't write.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: That's what I'm saying.

jokes.text: Giddyup.
jokes.setup: Kramer buying cowboy boots in Japan town
jokes.punchline: His characteristic cowboy exclamation after the purchase
jokes.explanation: Pure Kramer - the single word perfectly captures his character and the absurd situation.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Giddy up!

jokes.text: You're a cowboy now.
jokes.setup: Kramer buying cowboy boots
jokes.punchline: The salesman's matter-of-fact declaration of Kramer's new identity
jokes.explanation: Nice absurdist moment with the simple declaration carrying surprising weight.

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Salesman
dialogue.line: You are now cowboy.

jokes.text: Shh! ... Elaine, the song.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to talk during dinner
jokes.punchline: Brett shushing her because 'Desperado' is playing
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Brett's obsession, creating genuine relationship tension.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, I have something to tell you—

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Shh! Shh! 'Desperado' is on!

jokes.text: So you're angry that this bizarre carpet cabal made no attempt to abduct you?
jokes.setup: George complaining that the carpet cleaners didn't try to recruit him
jokes.punchline: Jerry's perfect summary with alliteration and the word 'cabal'
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay with 'carpet cabal' and perfect encapsulation of George's absurd complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. You're upset that the carpet cleaners didn't try to recruit you into their carpet cleaning cabal?

jokes.text: Please. Too dumb?
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting George looked too smart to brainwash
jokes.punchline: George's self-aware correction that he'd be too dumb, not too smart
jokes.explanation: Perfect George self-awareness - he knows exactly how others perceive him.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're too smart to brainwash.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I'd be too dumb. You can't brainwash somebody who's already a little soft in the head.

jokes.text: See, here, you're just another apple. But in Japan, you're an exotic fruit... like an orange, which is rare there.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Jerry should do a show in Japan
jokes.punchline: The fruit metaphor that treats oranges as exotic
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic from George, though the metaphor gets slightly convoluted.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what the problem is? You're thinking about this all wrong. In Japan, you'd be like an orange.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: An orange?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know how exotic oranges are in Japan? They're like a delicacy. They wrap 'em up in tissue paper, put 'em in a box... They're beautiful. You'd be like that. You'd be the orange.

jokes.text: You went out with my butler? Who said you could go out with my butler?
jokes.setup: The pilot show premise being explained to Japanese executives
jokes.punchline: Jerry's possessive outrage over 'his' butler dating
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the pilot with escalating possessiveness that's character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's my butler! You can't just date my butler!

jokes.text: No. That's what makes it such a humorous situation.
jokes.setup: Japanese executive asking if forced butlering is customary in America
jokes.punchline: George's meta-explanation of why the ridiculous premise is supposed to be funny
jokes.explanation: Great meta-commentary on sitcom logic delivered with George's desperate conviction.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, you see, in America we have this thing called 'the bit.' And the bit is that you take an absurd premise and you just run with it. So the humor comes from the fact that it's so ridiculous that nobody would ever actually do this in real life. But because we're doing it, it becomes funny. That's the whole structure of American comedy.

jokes.text: You've been living in America too long. You've forgotten what it's like to have no oranges.
jokes.setup: Japanese executive rejecting the pilot
jokes.punchline: George doubling down on his fruit metaphor as an explanation
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows George's inability to let go of a bad analogy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like a fruit. It's a kiwi. And a papaya had a baby. And that baby, when it grew up, it had a little fling with a mango. That mango left. Now it's with a grapefruit. The grapefruit leaves. Now it's back with the papaya. But it's not the same papaya!

jokes.text: What does he listen to, the all 'Desperado' station?
jokes.setup: Elaine complaining about getting shushed during the song
jokes.punchline: Jerry's exasperated question about Brett's music habits
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the running gag with Jerry's characteristic incredulity.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, he shushes you during the song?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What kind of a guy shushes someone during a song?

jokes.text: I like 'Witchy Woman.' ... Oh. Witch-eh Woman.
jokes.setup: Elaine suggesting a song she likes
jokes.punchline: Jerry's pronunciation correction that changes the meaning
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay that works but isn't particularly memorable or clever.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I love that song 'Joni Mitchell - Both Sides Now'.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's 'Beau Sides Now' - it's about a guy named Beau.

jokes.text: You have them sleeping in drawers?
jokes.setup: Learning Kramer's Japanese guests are comfortable in the chest of drawers
jokes.punchline: Jerry's horrified realization of the literal sleeping arrangement
jokes.explanation: Great escalation that reveals an absurd situation we hadn't fully grasped.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, they're sleeping in the dresser? Like, in the drawers?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, they seem very comfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer! You can't have people sleeping in your furniture! What if they get stuck? What if there's a fire?

jokes.text: This has international incident written all over it.
jokes.setup: Learning about Japanese businessmen sleeping in furniture drawers
jokes.punchline: Jerry's recognition of the potential diplomatic implications
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that takes the absurd situation to its logical extreme consequence.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So they sleep in drawers? That's actually kind of genius. I mean, think about it - we could solve the Middle East crisis. Just put all the world leaders in filing cabinets. Nobody can start a war if they're stuck in a drawer.

jokes.text: East meets West, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Serving Rice Krispies to Japanese businessmen sleeping in drawers
jokes.punchline: Kramer's grandiose description of this cultural fusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer line - treating mundane cereal as profound cultural exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Rice Krispies, the breakfast of champions! You got your snap, crackle, pop - that's Japanese efficiency right there. Snap! Crackle! Pop! It's like a Shinto shrine in a bowl.

jokes.text: No. It's mine.
jokes.setup: Elaine suggesting they share 'Desperado' as their song
jokes.punchline: Brett's possessive refusal to share his precious song
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Brett's selfishness extending to something as intangible as a song.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: No way! That's my song. I can't share 'Desperado.' That song means everything to me.

jokes.text: But where were you during the poncho craze of '84? I almost lost my house.
jokes.setup: Jerry reminiscing about their umbrella selling days
jokes.punchline: Teddy's dramatic accusation about abandoning him during a poncho crisis
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that makes umbrella selling sound like a life-or-death profession.

dialogue.speaker: Teddy
dialogue.line: You abandoned me during the poncho crisis!

jokes.text: Two hundred dollars. ... Special price for a real foul-weather friend.
jokes.setup: Jerry just wanting to buy an umbrella from his former colleague
jokes.punchline: Teddy's vindictive pricing with a weather-related pun on 'fair-weather friend'
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that perfectly captures the absurd revenge and professional bitterness.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How much for the umbrella?

dialogue.speaker: Teddy
dialogue.line: That'll be $200.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: $200? For an umbrella?

dialogue.speaker: Teddy
dialogue.line: Well, you were a fair-weather friend to me. Now you can be a fair-weather customer.

jokes.text: What kind of a snobby, stuck-up cult is this?
jokes.setup: The carpet cleaners still haven't tried to recruit George
jokes.punchline: George's indignant complaint about the cult's selectivity
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - only George would be offended by a cult's lack of interest in him.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What am I, not good enough for them? I'm insulted! They come to my apartment, they see my whole life, and they don't even try to recruit me? What, I'm not cult material?

jokes.text: It's not the money. It's my hand. It's crippled from writing and writing.
jokes.setup: Jerry's checks getting ruined in the rain
jokes.punchline: Jerry dramatically claiming his hand is crippled from signing tiny checks
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the running gag with appropriate dramatic overstatement.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My hand is crippled! I can't feel my fingers anymore. I've signed so many tiny checks, my hand has completely seized up. I'm like a claw. A useless claw. I can't even hold a pen now.

jokes.text: Boogeyman. Boogey? I'm quite sure.
jokes.setup: Elaine saying 'boogity man'
jokes.punchline: Brett's pretentious correction about the proper pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Brett's pedantic nature, though the joke itself is mild.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: boogity man

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: It's 'boogeyman,' not 'boogity man.'

jokes.text: You think she liked me?
jokes.setup: George having just pitched a TV show to Japanese executives
jokes.punchline: His immediate focus on whether a woman found him attractive
jokes.explanation: Perfect George character beat - his priorities revealed in the most inappropriate moment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what did she think? Did she seem interested?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, they just bought your show.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but did she think I was attractive?

jokes.text: Come on in, fat boy!
jokes.setup: Kramer greeting George at his apartment with Japanese businessmen
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual use of an insulting nickname in front of guests
jokes.explanation: Good Kramer moment showing his complete lack of social awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey, Giddy-up!

jokes.text: Pull harder. I'm trying. I can't get a grip. My hand's had kind of a bad week.
jokes.setup: Japanese businessman stuck in warped drawer
jokes.punchline: Jerry's understatement about his carpal tunnel preventing the rescue
jokes.explanation: Great callback that puts Jerry's trivial problem in the context of a genuine emergency.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I would help you, Mr. Tanaka, but I've got a little carpal tunnel thing going on.

jokes.text: Not the Farbman!
jokes.setup: Jerry about to axe the drawer to free the trapped businessman
jokes.punchline: Brett's horror at destroying the designer furniture over human safety
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation showing Brett's priorities - furniture over human welfare.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm going to have to destroy this drawer.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! That's a $6,000 Italian bedroom set!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: There's a man trapped in here.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: I don't care! You can't just destroy it!

jokes.text: Him, you brainwashed? What's he got that I don't have?!
jokes.setup: Discovering Mr. Wilhelm has been recruited by the carpet cleaning cult
jokes.punchline: George's jealous outrage at being passed over for recruitment
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to George's desire to be brainwashed - now he's genuinely jealous of a cult victim.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. They recruited Mr. Wilhelm? The carpet cleaning cult recruited Mr. Wilhelm and not me? What is he, some kind of carpet cleaning genius? I've been going to that dry cleaner for years!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is unbelievable. I'm funny, I'm charming, I've got material. And they want Wilhelm? Wilhelm doesn't even talk!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to go down there right now and demand they recruit me. If they want Wilhelm, they can have Wilhelm, but they're going to have to take me too.

jokes.text: My name is Tanya.
jokes.setup: George trying to deprogram Mr. Wilhelm
jokes.punchline: Wilhelm's complete transformation including a new feminine identity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the cult transformation is more complete and bizarre than expected.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Mr. Wilhelm, you've been brainwashed. We need to deprogram you.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Wilhelm
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: Just trust me. We're going to get you back to normal.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Wilhelm
dialogue.line: But I don't want to go back. I'm happy now. I'm going by Wilhelmina, and I've never felt better.

jokes.text: I didn't mean to hit you in the head with... an axe.
jokes.setup: Brett apologizing for the accident
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of hitting someone with an axe, even just the handle
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup with the absurd understatement of axe violence.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: I'm sorry about the accident with the axe.

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: You hit me with an axe?

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Just the handle.

jokes.text: Doctor, I think we're losing him. ... She got the moon in her eye
jokes.setup: Elaine's dramatic medical concern about Brett's condition
jokes.punchline: Brett 'dying' but actually just singing 'Witchy Woman'
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection that brings together multiple musical threads in the episode's climax.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Brett! Are you okay?!

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Rocking the cradle of love... Rocking the cradle of love...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's delirious! We need to call an ambulance!

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: She's a witch of the night... Rocking the cradle of love...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait... is that 'Witchy Woman'?

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Rocking the cradle of love...

tmdb_overview: Jerry's popularity in Japan brings new friends to Kramer, and maybe new life to Jerry's TV pilot; Elaine's new beau won't share "his song" with her.
insight: Fifty jokes in 22 minutes, but 74 score suggests escalation fatigue over impact.
